For each type of ﬁbre, Table 3 gives the dosages, in terms of mass per cubic meter and of volume percentage. In the following, the combinations ﬁbre-dosage will be indicated by the codes given in the ﬁrst column of Table 3 . For each ﬁbre-dosage combination, seven beams were cast and tested in a three-point bending scheme. Moreover, two cubes (150 /C2 150 /C2 150 mm3) and one cylinder (150 /C2 300 mm2) were also cast and used to measure the compressive strength and the elastic modulus of the FRCs at the time of testing, respectively. All the specimens were cast using the concrete mix given in Ta- ble 1 with the exception of the superplasticizer which in each case was adjusted in order to obtain a slump value in the range of 15– 17 cm. 3. Experimental results This section describes the results obtained during the experi- mental campaign. The fresh state and the mechanical properties of the concrete specimens under compression will be brieﬂy pre- sented and then the results of the three-point bending tests will be shown and discussed. 3.1. Fresh concrete properties In order to compare the mechanical properties of the different FRCs, the stability of the properties of the concrete matrix among different batches is extremely important. As already anticipated, the reference concrete was deﬁned according to EN 14845:2006. During the casting process, some properties of the fresh concrete were measured: slump (Abrams cone), hydration temperature (measured 5 min after the beginning of mixing), and mass. The val- ues are listed in Table 4 . They show very small variability among the different batches. For some of the FRCs, the workability was also measured using the Vebe test, which gave results of about 2 s. The properties of the fresh concrete were stable among all the batches. 3.2. Mechanical properties under compression For each mix, two cubes (150 /C2 150 /C2 150 mm3) were cast, and after 28 days of water curing, tested to obtain the compressive strength; the results are reported in Table 5 and Fig. 5 . The speci- mens showed very similar compressive strengths (see Fig. 5), with a mean value of 43.2 MPa and a coefﬁcient of variation of 7.0%. The only exceptions are the specimens of the set MS1_2, characterized by higher values of compressive strength. Excluding the data from these latter specimens the mean values becomes 42.2 MPa and the coefﬁcient of variation 3.1%. The elastic modulus of FRCs was measured as well, by compres- sion tests on 150 /C2 300 mm cylinders; the values are given in Ta- ble 5 . The elastic modulus for the cylinders of the sets MS3_2, MS3_4_8, SF_20 and SF_35 were not measured, and therefore the values measured during a parallel experimental campaign with the same FRCs are reported in Table 5. Also in this case the results are very stable if the specimens of the set MS1_2 are excluded (COV = 9.2% with MS1_2 data and COV = 2.8% without MS1_2 data). The number of specimens used for obtaining compressive strength and elastic modulus is small compared to that used for the three- Fig. 4. point bending tests described in the following. This small number was justiﬁed by the high stability of the results obtained during the preliminary campaign performed to deﬁne the reference concrete mix and during other experimental campaigns with the same FRCs. Furthermore, compressive strength and elastic modulus, for the ﬁ- bre dosages considered in this work, are almost independent of the dosage of ﬁbres (see Fig. 5 as an example) and, the high variability of the three-point bending test results described in the following is due to the uneven distribution of the ﬁbres in the specimens. 3.3. Results of the three-point bending tests Three-point bending tests were performed with load and CMOD measured (see Fig. 2 for the test set-up). Seven specimens were tested for each type of FRC. As an example, Fig. 6a shows the curves obtained from the specimens of the set SF_20 (20 kg/m 3 of steel ﬁ- bres) together with the force-CMOD curve of a plain-concrete spec- imen tested during the concrete-mix deﬁnition phase (dashed line). As widely known, the addition of the ﬁbres to the concrete does not signiﬁcantly increase the tensile strength, but greatly enhances the concrete toughness. It is worth noting that the scatter of the force-CMOD curves is usually signiﬁcant when test method pre- scribed by EN 14651:2005 is adopted. This is a consequence of the small number of ﬁbres crossing the fracture surface, and of its consequent high statistical variability due to the small size of the crack surface ( [15–20]). The scatter of the results in the three-point bending beam test strongly depends on the type and amount of ﬁbres, as can be seen by comparing the force-CMOD curves for the specimens of the set SF_20 (20 kg/m 3 of steel ﬁbres; Fig. 6 a) and the set MS3_2 (2 kg/m 3 of macro-synthetic ﬁbres; Fig. 6b). The scatter of the experimental results in the post-peak re- gion is much smaller for the specimens with the MS ﬁbres than for the SF specimens. As it will be discussed in the following, this is due to the higher number and the more homogeneous distribution of the MS ﬁbres over the fracture surface. In the following, the results of the tests will be given in concise form in order to allow the comparison of the performances of the different FRCs. The reference strength values indicated in Fig. 3 were extracted for the experimental curves and statistical analysis was performed. Fig. 7a shows the ﬂexural tensile strength at the limit of propor- tionality, f f ct;L, for the different specimens. The gray dots indicate the experimental data, while the thick and the thin black lines indi- cate, for each set of specimens, the mean value and the ±1 standard deviation ( r) interval, respectively. It is worth noting that, with only one exception (the specimens of the set MS1_2), the values of f f ct;L measured in each set are quite close and their mean values falls into the interval 4.3 ± 0.3 MPa, as required by EN 14845- 1:2006. As previously observed, the specimens of the set MS1_2 had slightly better mechanical properties. In order to better compare the results, here and in the following, the effects of the variability of the mechanical properties of the concrete have been reduced by normalizing the f f ct;L values with re- spect to the ﬂexural tensile strength value of each concrete, fct,ﬂ; predicted by Model Code 90 [21] as a function of the compressive strength of concrete (see Table 5 ). For a 125 mm high plain-con- crete-beam, the ﬂexural tensile str

Post-cracking behaviour of steel and macro-synthetic ﬁbre-reinforced concretes Nicola Buratti ⇑, Claudio Mazzotti, Marco Savoia DICAM-Structural Engineering, University of Bologna, Viale Risorgimento 2, 40136 Bologna, Italy article info Article history: Received 5 August 2010 Received in revised form 19 November 2010 Accepted 7 December 2010 Available online 7 January 2011 Keywords: Fibre-reinforced concrete Toughness Steel ﬁbres Macro-synthetic ﬁbres Three-point bending abstract The present paper describes the results of an experimental investigation on the performances of concrete specimens reinforced with either steel or macro-synthetic ﬁbres under three-point bending. Steel ﬁbres are often used to improve the ﬂexural toughness of concrete and are used in various structural applica- tions while synthetic ﬁbres are more often used to reduce crack opening due to shrinkage. Macro-syn- thetic ﬁbres have been proposed more recently with the aim of creating an alternative to steel ﬁbres in structural applications but their use is still limited. In the tests performed, specimens cast with the same concrete mix, but containing different dosages of either steel or macro-synthetic ﬁbres, were used and compared. In general steel ﬁbres were more efﬁ- cient in increasing the toughness of concrete than macro-synthetic ﬁbres, even though their results were signiﬁcantly more scattered. Test results were used to calculate the parameters of stress-crack opening relations via inverse analysis using a cracked hinge model. This numerical model provided results, in terms of force-Crack Mouth Opening Displacement (CMOD) curves, in very good agreement with the experimental data. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Fibre-reinforced concrete (FRC), obtained by adding steel or synthetic ﬁbres to plain-concrete, is becoming widely used in civil engineering because of its favourable mechanical properties [1,2]. In particular, ﬁbres give to concrete a signiﬁcant residual tensile strength in the cracked phase (toughness), due to the ﬁbre bridging mechanisms across the crack surfaces. The mechanical properties of FRC depend on the characteristics of the concrete matrix but also on the type and geometry of the ﬁ- bres, governing the bond mechanism between them and the con- crete [3]. Two main types of ﬁbres are commonly available to engineers: steel and synthetic ﬁbres. Steel ﬁbres are used in a wide range of structural applications such as industrial pavements [4,5], precast structural elements [6], tunnel linings [7,8], etc. and, in general, when the control of concrete cracking is important. Synthetic ﬁbres are usually smaller than steel ﬁbres and are most typically used in industrial pavements to reduce the cracking induced by shrinkage. In some applications, steel and synthetic ﬁbres are mixed together to obtain FRCs with better performance [9]. Recently, macro-syn- thetic ﬁbres have been produced with the aim of substituting steel ﬁbres in structural applications, but knowledge on the mechanical behaviour of concretes reinforced with these ﬁbres is still limited. This paper describes the results of an extensive experimental campaign performed at the Laboratory of Structural Engineering of University of Bologna. Several concrete prisms were cast using different types and dosages of steel and macro-synthetic ﬁbres and were tested in a three-point bending scheme. All the prisms were cast using the same concrete mix design (reference concr

2.1. Reference concrete The mechanical properties of the concrete used in the present study were set in agreement with the prescriptions of EN 14845:2006, which speciﬁes four types of reference concrete with given ﬂexural tensile strength, maximum size of aggregate and maximum cement content. The standard requires the ﬁbre manu- facturers to declare the performances of their product at least in one speciﬁc concrete mix among the four possible, which therefore becomes mandatory. For this mix, used in the present work, the standard speciﬁes a maximum aggregate size of 20 mm and a max- imum cement content of 350 kg/m 3. Moreover a ﬂexural tensile strength of 4.3 ± 0.3 MPa in a three-point bending test is required. In order to obtain a concrete with this ﬂexural tensile strength, some preliminary tests were performed, where specimens cast using twelve different mix designs were tested in a three-point bending scheme. These mixes were characterized by three differ- ent values of water/cement ratio and by four different types of aggregates. This phase of the study led to the deﬁnition of the reference concrete mix with the desired ﬂexural tensile strength. Table 1 gives the mix design and Fig. 1 the aggregate grading. 2.2. Test method The test procedure adopted was consistent with the guidelines given by EN 14651:2005 and EN 14845:2006: 150 /C2 150 /C2 550 (height /C2 width /C2 length) mm 3 prismatic specimens were tested in the three-point bending scheme shown in Fig. 2. The specimens were notched at the mid-span (the height of the notch is 25 mm), in order to control the triggering of the crack. The specimens were cured in moulds, under polyethylene sheeting, for 24 h after cast- ing and then demoulded and cured for a further 26 days under water at 20 /C176C. At the end of the curing period, the specimens were prepared for the test and tested 28 days after casting. The tensile behaviour of the FRCs was tested by recording load-Crack Mouth Opening Displacement (CMOD) curves, the latter being measured by a displacement transducer (see Fig. 2 ). The testing machine adopted operated under displacement control with a constant rate of displacement (CMOD or deﬂection), and had sufﬁcient stiffness to avoid unstable unloading phenomena in the softening branch of the load-CMOD curve. During the tests, the rate of increase of the CMOD was con- trolled, i.e., 0.05 mm/min for CMOD 6 0.1 mm and 0.2 mm/min for CMOD > 0.1 mm. All the tests were ended at CMOD = 4 mm. Fig. 3 shows a generic force-CMOD curve obtained from the beam tests. According to EN 14651:2005 the force-CMOD curve is characterized by some reference points: the load at the limit of proportionality, F L, deﬁned as the maximum load for CMOD 6 0.05 mm, and F1, F2, F3, F4, deﬁned as the load values at CMOD = 0.5 mm, 1.5 mm, 2.5 mm and 3.5 mm, respectively. These points describe the post-cracking behaviour of the FRC specimen. The results of the tests can be expressed also in terms of mid- span ﬂexural tensile stress, rt conventionally obtained by assum- ing a linear stress distribution over the mid-span cross section, i.e., rt = M/W, where M is the mid-span bending moment and W is the section modulus of the notched cross section. The ﬂexural tensile strength corresponding to FL is indicated as f f ct;L whereas those obtained from F1, F2, F3, F4, in the post-peak region of the force-CMOD curve, as f1, f2, f3, and f4, respectively. 2.3. Test program Four different types of ﬁbres were used: three

observed for the strength at the limit of proportionality, the scatter of the results, with respect to the mean value, is higher for the steel FRCs also as far as the residual strengths are concerned. Fig. 9 a shows the mean normalized residual strength for increasing values of the crack opening. The performance of the steel ﬁbres is usually better than those of synthetic ﬁbres if the mean values are considered. Nevertheless, in design problems the characteristic values (5% fractiles) are commonly used instead of the mean values, in order to take the scatter of the experimental data also into account. If the characteristic residual strengths of the different sets are compared, as done in Fig. 9b, the beneﬁt given by the steel ﬁbres is reduced. This result is mainly a consequence of the three-point bending test set-up (with small dimension of the fracture surface), which gives results with high scatter for this kind of ﬁbres [19]. Finally, the toughness index was calculated for each specimen. Since data for the plain-concrete specimens were available (col- lected during the preliminary phase), the toughness index was as- sumed as the ratio of the area under the force-CMOD curve up to CMOD = 4 mm for the FRC specimen over that for the plain-con- crete specimen [22]. For the latter, the mean value of the areas un- der the force-CMOD curves measured in the preliminary tests for the reference concrete was used. The toughness indexes obtained from the various tests on the specimens are shown in Fig. 10. As expected, the addition of ﬁbres, both steel and macro-synthetic, to the concrete increased its toughness from 5 to 10 times. The distribution of the values of the toughness index is very similar to that of the residual strengths. The toughness values for the steel FRC are higher than those of the (a) (b) Fig. 7. concretes with macro-synthetic ﬁbers, but they are signiﬁcantly scattered, especially as far as the smallest dosage of ﬁbres is concerned. 3.4. Data scattering in three-point bending tests In order to understand the reasons of the high scatter of the experimental results for some types of ﬁbres, the number of ﬁbres crossing the crack surface was determined. With this aim, the bending tests were carried out until failure, removing the trans- ducers when the CMOD increased over 4 mm. The crack surface of each specimen was divided into three horizontal strips with equal height (1/3 of the total height each), and the number of ﬁbres in each strip was counted. The ﬁbres on the two faces of the crack were then counted, distinguishing between broken and pulled-out ﬁbres. This procedure was complicated when dealing with the specimens of the set MS1 because of the very small cross section of the ﬁbres to their tendency to bundle. Fig. 11 shows, for each FRC type, the total number of ﬁbres crossing the crack surface of the various specimens. The mean val- ues (indicated by thick black lines) and the ±1 r intervals are also reported. It should be noted that, as far as the FRCs containing steel ﬁbres are concerned, the intervals for the two dosages considered are strongly overlapped. On the contrary, as far as the macro-syn- thetic ﬁbres are concerned, the ±1 r intervals corresponding to the two dosages are usually non-overlapping. These data were then used to ﬁnd a correlation between the residual strength values presented in the previous section and the number of ﬁbres in each strip of the surface. For the sake of brevity, here only the correlation with the total number of ﬁbres is considered. Fig. 12 gives the normalized ﬂexural tensile strength (a) (b) Fig. 9. deﬁned by a normalized function of the crack opening w, g(w), such that g(0) = 1. For FRCs, the shape of the stress-crack opening rela- tionship can be complex and signiﬁcantly inﬂuenced by the amount and type of ﬁbres used (e.g. [30]). The simplest approximation is a bilinear curve ( Fig. 14 a): gðwÞ¼ bi /C0 aiw ¼ 1 /C0 a1w; 0 /C20 w /C20 w1 b2 /C0 a2w; w1 /C20 w /C20 w2 /C26 ð3Þ where w1 and w2 denote the limits of the two linear branches of the stress-crack opening relationship. The deformation of the hinge is described by half the angular deformation / and the depth of the neutral axis y0 (see Fig. 14 b). Introducing the hinge length, s, the stress in the strip at the height y can be written as: rwðwðyÞÞ ¼ ð 2ðy /C0 y0Þ/ /C0 wðyÞÞ /C1 ð E=sÞ; ð4Þ where the associated crack-bridging stress is given in Eq. (2b). From Eqs. (2b) and (3) it is possible to write: rwðwðyÞÞ ¼ fct ðbi /C0 aiwðyÞÞ ð 5Þ with i = 1 for 0 6 w 6 w1 and i = 2 for w1 6 w 6 w2. If Eqs. (4) and (5) are solved with respect to w(y) and rw(w(y)), the following equa- tions are obtained: wðyÞ¼ 2ðy /C0 y0Þ/ /C0 fi 1 /C0 bi ð6Þ rwðwðyÞÞ ¼ fi /C0 2ðy /C0 y0Þ/bi 1 /C0 bi E s ð7Þ where the dimensionless parameters bi and fi are given by: bi ¼ fct ais E ; fi ¼ fct bis E ð8Þ Eqs. (6) and (7) establish in analytical form the crack opening proﬁle w(y) and the stress distribution rw(w(y)) in the cracked part of the hinge as a function of the hinge displacement parameters / and y0. According to Eq. (6), the crack opening proﬁle w(y) is de- ﬁned by linear branches related to the segments of the stress-crack opening relation (Eq. (3)). As the crack propagates from the bottom of the beam, the stress distribution changes following four subsequent phases ( Fig. 14 c). Phase 0 corresponds to the state of stress prior to cracking and Phases I–III to different states of stress during the crack propaga- tion. In each phase, Eqs. (6) and (7) give the shape of the crack opening and the corresponding bridging tensile stresses. Balancing the resultants of the sectional stresses with the external force N and the bending moment M acting on the cross section, a relation- ship between them and w is obtained in each phase. The crack width at different heights, w(y), can then be easily evaluated using Eq. (6). It is worth noting that Olesen’s cracked hinge model assumes that the compressive zone remains in the linear elastic range and the nonlinear behaviour is concentrated in the cracked zone. The linear behaviour of the compressive zone is assumed in many cracked hinge models such as those proposed by Pedersen [33], Casanova and Rossi [34], Rossi [27] and RILEM TC 162-TDF [25], which compare favourably with nonlinear FEM analysis [25]. This approximation allows simple solutions that can be easily used in design and derivation an explicit solution in the case of Olesen’s model. In the general case where a nonlinear stress–strain relation for the concrete in compression is chosen, a numerical integration of the stresses over the depth of the cross section can be carried out. 4.2. Inverse analysis formulation An inverse analysis procedure has been used in order to ﬁnd the values of the parameters deﬁning the stress-crack opening relation, i.e., ft, r(w1), w1 and w2 (see Fig. 14 a) that, together with Olesen’s model, allow best ﬁt to the experimental results. The length of the nonlinear hinge could be considered, in principle, a ﬁtting parameter as well because, in general, it depends on the type of str

Good accuracy of the simulated results for all the specimens subjected to testing has been obtained. In particular, the stress- crack opening relations calibrated by the adopted procedure, to- gether with Olesen’s model, give force-CMOD diagrams in good agreement with the experimental diagrams in terms of all the quantities considered in the optimization process. The error, is very small as far as the force at the limit of proportionality and the area under the curve are concerned (less than 2% and than 1%, respec- tively), while they are slightly greater in terms of residual strengths: up to 15% for f1 and up to 5% for f2, f3, and f4. In order to facilitate the comparison of the results, Fig. 15 shows the mean stress-crack opening relationship for each set of speci- mens. Consistently with what observed previously, the steel ﬁbres give slightly better performances with respect to macro-synthetic ﬁbres. The matching of the numerical curves with the experimental data is in some cases less accurate in the curve portion just after the peak: the steeper the curve, the lower the matching. As an example, Fig. 16a–b shows a comparison between the numerical and the experimental force – CMOD diagrams for one specimen of the set named SF_35. In this case, the model provides very good predictions (Fig. 16a) except for very low crack openings, the accu- racy of the results can be lower (see Fig. 16 b). 5. Conclusions The present work investigated the ﬂexural behaviour of notched beams made of concretes reinforced with different types and dos- ages of macro-synthetic and steel ﬁbres. Steel ﬁbres showed gener- ally better performance than macro-synthetic ﬁbres. In fact, given the same number of ﬁbres crossing the crack propagation surface, steel ﬁbres provided higher toughness than macro-synthetic ﬁbres. It is important to note that for the type of specimens considered here (150 /C2 150 /C2 550 mm3), the actual number of ﬁbres crossing the crack is not always strongly related to the nominal dosage of ﬁbres. In fact, for a given nominal dosage of ﬁbres of either macro-synthetic or steel ﬁbres, the scatter of the number of ﬁbres crossing the crack surface is very high. It has been shown that this scatter is a consequence of the test set-up used. In fact, due to the small size of the crack surface with respect to the length of the ﬁbres, the number of ﬁbres in the cracked section can be very different among various specimens ob- tained from the same concrete batch.
